---
title: "Spatial Data Integration"
subtitle: "Module 3 | PAF 516 — Community Analytics"
author: "Arizona State University"
format:
  beamer:
    theme: Madrid
    colortheme: beaver
    fonttheme: professionalfonts
    slide-level: 2
    aspectratio: 169
    toc: false
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \definecolor{asuMaroon}{HTML}{8C1D40}
        \definecolor{asuGold}{HTML}{FFC627}
        \setbeamercolor{palette primary}{bg=asuMaroon,fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{palette secondary}{bg=asuGold,fg=black}
        \setbeamercolor{palette tertiary}{bg=asuMaroon!80,fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{frametitle}{bg=asuMaroon,fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{title}{fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{block title}{bg=asuMaroon,fg=white}
        \setbeamercolor{alerted text}{fg=asuMaroon}
        \setbeamercolor{section title}{fg=white}
        \setbeamertemplate{footline}[frame number]
        \setbeamerfont{frametitle}{size=\large,series=\bfseries}
        \usepackage{booktabs}
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# Part 1: CRS, Spatial Joins & Buffer Analysis

## Module Overview

> "Your economic hardship index measures *who lives* in a place. This module adds *what is near* a place."

In Labs 1–2 you built the index entirely from census variables. This module teaches you to combine census data with **external point data** — EPA facilities, grocery stores, transit stops — using spatial operations to test environmental justice hypotheses.

**What you will learn:**

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{p{6cm}p{6cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Topic} & \textbf{Key Tool} \\
\midrule
Coordinate Reference Systems & \texttt{st\_crs()}, \texttt{st\_transform()} \\
Point-in-polygon spatial joins & \texttt{st\_join()} \\
Buffer analysis & \texttt{st\_buffer()} \\
Buffer sensitivity (UGCoP) & Kwan (2012) \\
Environmental justice framework & Maantay, Pastor et al. \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

## Coordinate Reference Systems

Before combining spatial datasets, they must share the same **CRS**.

**Geographic CRS (EPSG:4326 — WGS84)**

- Latitude/longitude in degrees
- Global — good for web maps and data exchange
- Distances distorted (1 degree ≠ constant length)
- Default for most downloaded data

**Projected CRS (EPSG:2868 — Arizona Central)**

- Flat surface with linear units (US survey feet)
- Accurate distances and areas in Maricopa County
- Required for `st_buffer()` to produce meaningful distances

::: {.callout-warning}
`st_buffer(data, dist = 1000)` on EPSG:4326 creates a buffer of 1000 **degrees** — roughly 100,000 km. Always project first.
:::

## CRS Workflow in R

**Key functions:**

- `st_crs()` — check the current CRS of any spatial object
- `st_transform(data, crs = 2868)` — project to Arizona Central (US survey feet)
- `st_make_valid()` — fix minor topology errors before spatial operations

**The rule:** Project → spatial operations → convert back to WGS84 for visualization.

::: {.callout-warning}
Always check CRS **before** combining datasets. Mismatched CRS = wrong results.
:::

## Spatial Joins: st_join()

A spatial join attaches attributes from one layer to another based on **geographic relationship** — not a shared column.

**Point-in-polygon** (most common in community analytics):

- `st_join(polygons, points, join = st_intersects)` — attaches point attributes to the polygon they fall within
- After joining, use `group_by()` + `summarise()` to count points per polygon
- Use `left_join()` to merge counts back to the original polygon layer

**Example:** How many EPA facilities fall within each block group? The spatial join answers this without needing a shared ID column.

## Buffer Analysis: st_buffer()

Buffers create a **proximity zone** around a spatial feature.

**Key functions:**

- `st_buffer(points, dist = 5280)` — creates a 1-mile buffer (EPSG:2868 uses US survey feet)
- `st_union()` — dissolves overlapping buffers into one combined zone
- `st_intersects()` — tests which polygons fall within the buffer (binary: exposed/not exposed)

**Common applications:**

- Block groups within 1 mile of a hazardous facility
- Grocery stores within 0.5 miles of each neighborhood centroid
- Hospital service area coverage

**Remember:** Distance units depend on CRS. EPSG:2868 = US survey feet. EPSG:4326 = degrees (not useful).

# Part 2: The UGCoP

## What Is UGCoP?

> *Kwan, M.P. (2012). The uncertain geographic context problem. Annals of the AAG, 102(5), 958–968.*
> doi:10.1080/00045608.2012.687349

**Core argument:**

> "The actual geographic context that influences people's behavior and health is **uncertain** and cannot be directly observed."

In practice:

- The researcher must choose a buffer size: 0.25 mi? 0.5 mi? 1 mi? 2 mi?
- There is rarely a principled theoretical basis for this choice
- Different buffer sizes change which units are classified as "exposed"
- Different classifications produce different statistical results

::: {.callout-important}
Buffer distance choice is a methodological decision — not a neutral technical step.
:::

## UGCoP vs. MAUP

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{p{3cm}p{5.5cm}p{5.5cm}}
\toprule
 & \textbf{MAUP} & \textbf{UGCoP} \\
\midrule
\textbf{What varies} & Polygon boundaries & Buffer radius \\
\textbf{Mechanism} & Aggregation changes statistics & Exposure zone changes who is exposed \\
\textbf{Classic example} & County vs.\ tract vs.\ block group & 0.5 mi vs.\ 1 mi vs.\ 2 mi \\
\textbf{Named by} & Openshaw \& Taylor (1979) & Kwan (2012) \\
\textbf{Applies to} & Polygon analysis & Proximity analysis \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

Both MAUP and UGCoP share one insight: **geographic context is a methodological choice**, and that choice shapes results.

## Buffer Sensitivity in Practice

Three block groups at different distances from an EPA facility:

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{lccc}
\toprule
\textbf{Buffer} & \textbf{A (0.3 mi)} & \textbf{B (0.7 mi)} & \textbf{C (1.1 mi)} \\
\midrule
0.5 mi & \textcolor{red}{Exposed} & --- & --- \\
1.0 mi & \textcolor{red}{Exposed} & \textcolor{red}{Exposed} & --- \\
2.0 mi & \textcolor{red}{Exposed} & \textcolor{red}{Exposed} & \textcolor{red}{Exposed} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

Same block groups, **three different classifications** — this is UGCoP.

**Lab 3, Q2:** Change distance bands and compare. This tests whether conclusions are threshold-sensitive.

## Best Practices for Buffer Analysis

How to address UGCoP:

1. **Use a theoretically grounded distance** — air dispersion models, walkability studies, and physical hazard decay rates each suggest specific distances
2. **Report results at multiple distances** — if conclusions hold at 0.5, 1.0, and 1.5 miles, the result is robust; if only one distance produces findings, flag the sensitivity
3. **Match distances from comparable studies** — Maantay (2002) and EPA EJScreen use facility-specific distances; consistency aids comparability
4. **Be explicit in reporting** — state the buffer distance, justify the choice, acknowledge sensitivity

::: {.callout-tip}
A result that holds across multiple buffer distances is far more credible than one that only appears at a single threshold.
:::

# Part 3: Environmental Justice

## The Environmental Justice Framework

EJ research asks: are environmental burdens disproportionately concentrated in low-income and minority communities?

**The three-part analysis:**

\begin{center}
\small
\begin{tabular}{p{4.5cm}p{4.5cm}p{4cm}}
\toprule
\textbf{Component} & \textbf{Data Source} & \textbf{Tool} \\
\midrule
Demographics --- who lives here & ACS census variables & \texttt{tidycensus} \\
Hazards --- what is near here & EPA TRI, Superfund listings & Point data \\
Spatial integration --- overlap? & Combined layers & \texttt{st\_join()}, \texttt{st\_buffer()} \\
\bottomrule
\end{tabular}
\end{center}

Lab 3 operationalizes this by combining 5 census variables measuring socioeconomic hardship with spatial proximity measures to environmental burden locations.

## Pastor, Sadd & Hipp (2001)

> *Which came first? Toxic facilities, minority move-in, and environmental justice. Journal of Urban Affairs, 23(1), 1–21.*

**The central EJ research question:**

- Were hazardous facilities **sited** in minority neighborhoods? *(disproportionate siting)*
- Or did minorities **move in** after facilities were built? *(market dynamics)*

**Key findings:**

- Used longitudinal data for LA County census tracts (1970–1990)
- Applied buffer-based proximity measures to track siting over time
- Minority and low-income communities had disproportionate exposure **before** move-in
- **Supports disproportionate siting hypothesis** over residential sorting
- Uses point-in-polygon joins + buffers — the same tools in Lab 3

## UGCoP in EJ Research

Maantay (2002) and Mohai & Saha (2015) both document how distance threshold choice affects EJ findings:

- **Results can flip across distances** — a community appears "exposed" at 1 mile but not at 0.5 miles, or vice versa
- **No single distance is universally correct** — air, water, and noise hazards have different physical decay rates
- **Smaller buffers concentrate findings** — a 0.25-mile buffer identifies only immediately adjacent communities
- **Policy consequences** — if a 1-mile buffer determines EJ remediation funding eligibility, the threshold choice has direct resource allocation implications

::: {.callout-warning}
**Lab 3, Q3** asks you to connect buffer sensitivity to UGCoP and cite Maantay (2002) and Mohai & Saha (2015). This is the theoretical payoff of Q2's empirical test.
:::

## Module 3 Summary

**Key takeaways:**

- **CRS before everything.** Mismatched coordinate systems produce wrong results. Always check and transform before spatial operations.
- **Spatial joins count by location.** `st_join()` attaches attributes using geographic relationship — no shared column needed.
- **Buffers define exposure.** `st_buffer()` creates proximity zones. The distance threshold is consequential.
- **UGCoP: buffers have a problem too.** Kwan (2012): different distances produce different classifications. Test multiple distances and report sensitivity.
- **EJ connects burden to hardship.** Spatial integration reveals whether environmental hazards compound census-measured disadvantage in the same communities.

**Coming up in Module 4:** Spatial Autocorrelation — Moran's I, LISA hot spots, and whether hardship clusters spatially across Maricopa County.
